1:
"Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, and have not charity, I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal." The witnesses note that the gift of tongues without love is worthless. Gill notes "the tongues of men" are all the languages men speak; Lightfoot discusses "the tongues of angels." The witnesses note Paul declaring that, though he speak with the tongues of men and of angels, without charity (love) he is but sounding brass or a tinkling cymbal; the supremacy of love over the most exalted gift of utterance — that the gift of tongues, even raised to the highest conceivable pitch (the speech of angels), is, without love, mere empty noise, a hollow clanging that profits nothing; love above tongues. The witnesses note the tongues of men and of angels. The witnesses note as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal.

The witnesses note that tongues without love is but sounding brass — the supremacy of love over the most exalted gift of utterance.

So the witnesses make the verse the worthlessness of tongues without love. The lesson the expositors draw is the supremacy of love over the most exalted gift of utterance, which without love is empty noise. The witnesses note the tongues: "though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels" — Gill notes the tongues of men are all languages; the tongues of angels the highest conceivable speech; the tongues; the men and angels; the utterance; the highest; the gift. The expositors observe the lack: "and have not charity" — without charity (love); the lack; the no charity; the without love; the missing; the love absent. The witnesses press the worthlessness: "I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal" — without love, he is but sounding brass or a tinkling (clanging) cymbal; the worthlessness; the sounding brass; the tinkling cymbal; the empty noise; the hollow. The expositors draw the lesson on the supremacy of love: love is supreme over the most exalted gift — tongues, even at the highest pitch (angels' speech), is without love mere noise; the supremacy; the love above; the tongues; the highest; the noise. The witnesses observe the empty noise: without love, the gift is empty noise — a hollow clanging that profits nothing; the empty noise; the hollow; the clanging; the profits nothing; the worthless. The expositors note the rebuke to the Corinthians: the Corinthians prized tongues above all — Paul shows that, without love, it is nothing; the rebuke; the tongues prized; the without love nothing; the Corinthians; the order. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the tongues of men and of angels, the sounding brass, and the supremacy of love. The verse thus records the worthlessness of tongues without love — teaching the supremacy of love over the most exalted gift of utterance, which without love is empty noise.
2:
"And though I have the gift of prophecy, and understand all mysteries, and all knowledge; and though I have all faith, so that I could remove mountains, and have not charity, I am nothing." The witnesses note that prophecy, knowledge, and faith without love are nothing. Gill notes the gift of prophecy (foretelling, or expounding, or preaching) — as Balaam and Caiaphas had it without love; so too the highest knowledge and the faith of miracles. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, though he have prophecy, all mysteries, all knowledge, and all faith to remove mountains, without charity he is nothing; the supremacy of love over the gifts of revelation and power — that even prophecy, the deepest understanding of the mysteries, the fullest knowledge, and the faith that moves mountains, if without love, leave a man nothing (not merely his gifts, but himself, worthless before God); love above prophecy, knowledge, and faith. The witnesses note understand all mysteries, and all knowledge. The witnesses note have not charity, I am nothing.

The witnesses note that prophecy, all knowledge, and mountain-moving faith without love leave a man nothing — love above the gifts of revelation and power.

So the witnesses make the verse the nothingness of gifts without love. The lesson the expositors draw is the supremacy of love over the gifts of revelation and power, which without love leave a man nothing. The witnesses note the prophecy and knowledge: "the gift of prophecy, and understand all mysteries, and all knowledge" — Gill notes prophecy (foretelling, expounding, preaching), as Balaam and Caiaphas had it without love; the deepest understanding; the prophecy; the mysteries; the knowledge; the revelation. The expositors observe the faith: "though I have all faith, so that I could remove mountains" — the faith of miracles, that could remove mountains (Matthew 17:20); the faith; the miracles; the remove mountains; the power; the gift. The witnesses press the nothingness: "and have not charity, I am nothing" — without charity, he is nothing; the nothingness; the I am nothing; the without love; the worthless; the nothing. The expositors draw the lesson on the supremacy of love: love is supreme over the gifts of revelation (prophecy, mysteries, knowledge) and power (faith) — without it, a man is nothing; the supremacy; the love above; the revelation; the power; the nothing. The witnesses observe the man himself nothing: without love, not merely the gifts but the man himself is nothing — worthless before God; the man himself; the nothing; the worthless; the before God; the not the gifts only. The expositors note the gifts without grace: the gifts (even the highest) may be possessed without grace (love) — as Balaam, Caiaphas, Judas; the gifts without grace; the Balaam; the Caiaphas; the without love; the nothing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the understand all mysteries and all knowledge, the have not charity I am nothing, and the supremacy of love. The verse thus records the nothingness of gifts without love — teaching the supremacy of love over the gifts of revelation and power, which without love leave a man nothing.
3:
"And though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor, and though I give my body to be burned, and have not charity, it profiteth me nothing." The witnesses note that the greatest works of charity and martyrdom without love profit nothing. Gill notes the Jews gave instances of those who distributed all their goods to the poor; and Paul speaks even of giving the body to be burned (martyrdom) — yet without love these profit nothing. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, though he give all his goods to the poor and his body to be burned, without charity it profits him nothing; the supremacy of love over the costliest deeds — that even the most extreme outward works (giving away all in alms, suffering martyrdom by fire), if without love (done from vainglory, or any motive but love), profit the doer nothing before God; love above almsgiving and martyrdom. The witnesses note bestow all my goods to feed the poor. The witnesses note give my body to be burned... profiteth nothing.

The witnesses note that giving all to the poor and even martyrdom without love profit nothing — love above the costliest deeds.

So the witnesses make the verse the unprofitableness of the costliest deeds without love. The lesson the expositors draw is the supremacy of love over the most extreme outward works, which without love profit nothing. The witnesses note the almsgiving: "though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor" — Gill notes the instances of those who gave all to the poor; the almsgiving; the all my goods; the the poor; the giving; the charity. The expositors observe the martyrdom: "though I give my body to be burned" — even giving the body to be burned (martyrdom by fire); the martyrdom; the body burned; the suffering; the extreme; the death. The witnesses press the no profit: "and have not charity, it profiteth me nothing" — without charity, it profits nothing; the no profit; the profiteth nothing; the without love; the worthless; the nothing. The expositors draw the lesson on the supremacy of love: love is supreme over the costliest deeds — even almsgiving of all and martyrdom, without love, profit nothing; the supremacy; the love above; the almsgiving; the martyrdom; the nothing. The witnesses observe the motive: the works without love may be done from vainglory, or any motive but love — and so profit nothing before God; the motive; the vainglory; the not love; the before God; the nothing. The expositors note the profit to the doer: the works without love profit the doer nothing — though they may benefit others, they earn him nothing before God; the profit; the doer; the nothing; the before God; the unprofitable. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the bestow all my goods to feed the poor, the give my body to be burned profiteth nothing, and the supremacy of love. The verse thus records the unprofitableness of the costliest deeds without love — teaching the supremacy of love over the most extreme outward works, which without love profit nothing.
4:
"Charity suffereth long, and is kind; charity envieth not; charity vaunteth not itself, is not puffed up," The witnesses note the first marks of love's character. Chrysostom notes that, having shown love requisite to the gifts and the virtues, Paul now outlines its matchless beauty, adorning its image with the parts of virtue as with colours — bidding us examine each with care, to know the treasure in it. The witnesses note Paul beginning the portrait of love — it suffers long, is kind, envies not, vaunts not itself, is not puffed up; the character of love drawn in its active graces — that love is patient under wrong (suffers long), benevolent (kind), free of envy at others' good, free of boasting (vaunteth not), and free of pride (not puffed up); the marks of love's character. The witnesses note charity suffereth long, and is kind. The witnesses note envieth not... is not puffed up.

The witnesses note love's first marks — long-suffering, kind, not envious, not boastful, not puffed up — the portrait of love in its active graces.

So the witnesses make the verse the first marks of love's character. The lesson the expositors draw is the portrait of love in its active graces — patient, kind, free of envy, boasting, and pride. The witnesses note Chrysostom's note on the portrait: Chrysostom notes Paul outlines love's matchless beauty, adorning its image with the parts of virtue as with colours, bidding us examine each; the portrait; the beauty; the virtues; the colours; the examine. The expositors observe the long-suffering and kindness: "charity suffereth long, and is kind" — love suffers long (is patient under wrong, slow to anger) and is kind (benevolent, gentle); the long-suffering; the patient; the kind; the benevolent; the gentle. The witnesses press the no envy: "charity envieth not" — love envies not (grieves not at others' good, prosperity, or gifts); the no envy; the not grieving; the others' good; the content; the love. The expositors draw the lesson on no boasting: "charity vaunteth not itself" — love vaunts not itself (boasts not, is not ostentatious); the no boasting; the not vaunting; the not ostentatious; the humble; the love. The witnesses observe the no pride: "is not puffed up" — love is not puffed up (not proud, not conceited); the no pride; the not puffed up; the not conceited; the humble; the love. The expositors note the active graces: these are love's active graces — patience, kindness; and its freedom from the vices — envy, boasting, pride; the active graces; the patience; the kindness; the freedom from vice; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the suffereth long and is kind, the envieth not is not puffed up, and the marks of love. The verse thus records the first marks of love's character — teaching the portrait of love in its active graces, patient, kind, free of envy, boasting, and pride.
5:
"Doth not behave itself unseemly, seeketh not her own, is not easily provoked, thinketh no evil;" The witnesses note further marks of love's character. Gill notes love does not behave unseemly — uses no unbecoming words or indecent actions, is careful no corrupt communication proceed from the mouth, nor ridiculous gestures that grieve the saints. The witnesses note Paul continuing the portrait — love behaves not unseemly, seeks not her own, is not easily provoked, thinks no evil; the further graces of love — that love is seemly (courteous, decorous), unselfish (seeks not her own interest), even-tempered (not easily provoked to anger), and guileless (thinks no evil, harbours no suspicion or grudge); the further marks of love. The witnesses note seeketh not her own. The witnesses note is not easily provoked, thinketh no evil.

The witnesses note love's further marks — seemly, unselfish, not easily provoked, thinking no evil — the portrait of love continued.

So the witnesses make the verse the further marks of love's character. The lesson the expositors draw is the portrait of love continued — seemly, unselfish, even-tempered, and guileless. The witnesses note the seemliness: "doth not behave itself unseemly" — Gill notes love uses no unbecoming words or indecent actions, no corrupt communication; the seemliness; the not unseemly; the courteous; the decorous; the becoming. The expositors observe the unselfishness: "seeketh not her own" — love seeks not her own (interest, profit, advantage), but the good of others; the unselfishness; the not her own; the others' good; the selfless; the love. The witnesses press the not provoked: "is not easily provoked" — love is not easily provoked (to anger, irritation, resentment); the not provoked; the even-tempered; the not angry; the patient; the love. The expositors draw the lesson on thinking no evil: "thinketh no evil" — love thinks no evil (harbours no suspicion, no grudge, devises no harm, imputes not evil motives); the no evil; the no suspicion; the no grudge; the guileless; the love. The witnesses observe the guilelessness: love is guileless — thinking no evil, harbouring no resentment, devising no harm; the guileless; the no evil thought; the no resentment; the no harm; the love. The expositors note the further graces: these are love's further graces — seemliness, unselfishness, even temper, guilelessness; the further graces; the seemly; the unselfish; the even-tempered; the guileless. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the seeketh not her own, the is not easily provoked thinketh no evil, and the further marks of love. The verse thus records the further marks of love's character — teaching the portrait of love continued, seemly, unselfish, even-tempered, and guileless.
6:
"Rejoiceth not in iniquity, but rejoiceth in the truth;" The witnesses note love's relation to iniquity and truth. Gill notes love rejoices not in iniquity — neither in its own nor in others'; but mourns over its own sins and is grieved at the sins of the world and of professors; nor does it rejoice in any injustice done to any, even an enemy. The witnesses note Paul declaring that love rejoices not in iniquity, but rejoices in the truth; the moral discernment of love — that love takes no pleasure in sin (whether its own, others', or any wrong done to any), but grieves over it, and instead rejoices in the truth (the gospel, righteousness, the good and right); love's joy in truth, not iniquity. The witnesses note rejoiceth not in iniquity. The witnesses note rejoiceth in the truth.

The witnesses note love rejoicing not in iniquity but in the truth — love's moral discernment, grieving over sin, delighting in righteousness.

So the witnesses make the verse love's relation to iniquity and truth. The lesson the expositors draw is love's moral discernment — taking no pleasure in sin, but rejoicing in the truth. The witnesses note the no rejoicing in iniquity: "rejoiceth not in iniquity" — Gill notes love rejoices not in iniquity, neither its own nor others'; the no rejoicing; the iniquity; the not own nor others'; the no pleasure; the sin. The expositors observe the grief over sin: love grieves over sin — mourning its own, grieved at the sins of the world and of professors; the grief; the mourning; the own sins; the world's sins; the grieved. The witnesses press the rejoicing in truth: "but rejoiceth in the truth" — love rejoices in the truth (the gospel, righteousness, the good and right); the rejoicing; the truth; the gospel; the righteousness; the joy. The expositors draw the lesson on love's moral discernment: love has moral discernment — taking no pleasure in sin or wrong, but delighting in truth and righteousness; the moral discernment; the no pleasure in sin; the delight in truth; the righteousness; the love. The witnesses observe no joy in wrong: love takes no joy in any injustice done to any (even an enemy) — but grieves at all wrong; the no joy in wrong; the injustice; the even an enemy; the grieved; the love. The expositors note the joy in truth: love rejoices in the truth — in the gospel's spread, in righteousness, in the good of others; the joy; the truth; the gospel; the righteousness; the good. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the rejoiceth not in iniquity, the rejoiceth in the truth, and love's moral discernment. The verse thus records love's relation to iniquity and truth — teaching love's moral discernment, taking no pleasure in sin but rejoicing in the truth.
7:
"Beareth all things, believeth all things, hopeth all things, endureth all things." The witnesses note the four-fold endurance of love. Gill notes love "beareth all things" — the burdens of fellow Christians, the infirmities of the weak, the reproaches of the world; or "covereth all things," even a multitude of sins, by forgiving freely. The witnesses note Paul completing the portrait — love bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things; the matchless patience and charity of love — that love bears (covers, supports) all wrongs and burdens, believes the best (is not suspicious), hopes for good even where it doubts, and endures all trials and persecutions; love's four-fold "all things." The witnesses note beareth all things, believeth all things. The witnesses note hopeth all things, endureth all things.

The witnesses note love's four-fold endurance — bears, believes, hopes, endures all things — the matchless patience and charity of love.

So the witnesses make the verse love's four-fold endurance. The lesson the expositors draw is the matchless patience and charity of love — bearing, believing, hoping, and enduring all things. The witnesses note the bearing: "beareth all things" — Gill notes love bears (or covers) all things — the burdens of fellow Christians, the infirmities of the weak, the reproaches of the world; the bearing; the covering; the burdens; the infirmities; the reproaches. The expositors observe the believing: "believeth all things" — love believes all things (the best, where it can; is not suspicious, not censorious); the believing; the the best; the not suspicious; the charitable; the trust. The witnesses press the hoping: "hopeth all things" — love hopes all things (for good, even where it doubts; hopes the best for the erring); the hoping; the for good; the the best; the even doubting; the hope. The expositors draw the lesson on the enduring: "endureth all things" — love endures all things (trials, persecutions, wrongs, to the end); the enduring; the trials; the persecutions; the to the end; the endurance. The witnesses observe the matchless charity: love's four-fold "all things" shows its matchless charity — bearing, believing, hoping, enduring without limit; the matchless; the four-fold; the all things; the without limit; the charity. The expositors note the covering of sins: love covers all things — forgiving freely, hiding a multitude of sins (1 Peter 4:8); the covering; the forgiving; the multitude of sins; the freely; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the beareth all things believeth all things, the hopeth all things endureth all things, and love's four-fold endurance. The verse thus records love's four-fold endurance — teaching the matchless patience and charity of love, bearing, believing, hoping, and enduring all things.
8:
"Charity never faileth: but whether there be prophecies, they shall fail; whether there be tongues, they shall cease; whether there be knowledge, it shall vanish away." The witnesses note the permanence of love against the cessation of the gifts. Chrysostom notes Paul points out love's worth from a fresh head — persuading those accounted inferior that it is in their power to have the chief of all, and they will be no worse off than the gifted, but much better. The witnesses note Paul declaring that charity never fails, but prophecies shall fail, tongues shall cease, and knowledge shall vanish away; the supremacy of love in its permanence — that love abides forever, while the spiritual gifts (prophecy, tongues, knowledge), being for the present imperfect state, shall cease when their purpose is fulfilled, so that love outlasts the very gifts the Corinthians prized; love never faileth. The witnesses note charity never faileth. The witnesses note prophecies shall fail, tongues shall cease.

The witnesses note love's permanence against the cessation of the gifts — love abides forever, while prophecy, tongues, and knowledge shall cease.

So the witnesses make the verse love's permanence against the gifts' cessation. The lesson the expositors draw is the supremacy of love in its permanence — love abiding forever, while the gifts cease. The witnesses note the permanence: "charity never faileth" — love never fails (never ceases, abides forever); the permanence; the never faileth; the abides; the forever; the love. The expositors observe the cessation of prophecy: "whether there be prophecies, they shall fail" — prophecies shall fail (cease, be done away); the prophecy; the shall fail; the cease; the done away; the gift. The witnesses press the cessation of tongues and knowledge: "whether there be tongues, they shall cease; whether there be knowledge, it shall vanish away" — tongues shall cease, knowledge (the gift) shall vanish away; the tongues; the knowledge; the cease; the vanish; the gifts. The expositors draw the lesson on love outlasting the gifts: love outlasts the very gifts the Corinthians prized — for the gifts are for the present imperfect state, but love abides; the outlasting; the love abides; the gifts cease; the imperfect state; the permanence. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's note on the inferior: Chrysostom notes love is in the power of those accounted inferior — they may have the chief of all (love), and be better off than the gifted; the inferior; the in their power; the chief of all; the better; the love. The expositors note the gifts for the present state: the gifts (prophecy, tongues, knowledge) are for the present, imperfect state — they cease when their purpose is fulfilled (v.10); the gifts; the present state; the imperfect; the cease; the purpose. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the charity never faileth, the prophecies shall fail tongues shall cease, and love's permanence. The verse thus records love's permanence against the gifts' cessation — teaching the supremacy of love in its permanence, love abiding forever while the gifts cease.
9:
"For we know in part, and we prophesy in part." The witnesses note the partial nature of present knowledge and prophecy. Gill notes this means not that the Scriptures are imperfect, nor that only some saints know, but that the saints' knowledge in this life is partial and imperfect — they know but in part, and prophesy (preach, expound) but in part. The witnesses note Paul declaring that we know in part and prophesy in part; the imperfection of present spiritual attainment — that the believer's knowledge and the prophet's gift, however real, are partial and fragmentary in this life, mere portions of the full understanding reserved for the world to come; the partial knowledge of the present. The witnesses note we know in part. The witnesses note we prophesy in part.

The witnesses note that we know and prophesy only in part — the imperfection of present spiritual attainment, mere portions of the full to come.

So the witnesses make the verse the partial nature of present knowledge. The lesson the expositors draw is the imperfection of present spiritual attainment, mere portions of the full understanding to come. The witnesses note the partial knowledge: "we know in part" — Gill notes the saints' knowledge in this life is partial and imperfect; the partial; the in part; the imperfect; the knowledge; the fragmentary. The expositors observe the partial prophecy: "we prophesy in part" — the prophet's gift (preaching, expounding) is also partial; the prophecy; the in part; the preaching; the partial; the gift. The witnesses press the imperfection: the believer's knowledge and the prophet's gift, however real, are partial and fragmentary in this life; the imperfection; the partial; the fragmentary; the this life; the incomplete. The expositors draw the lesson on the present imperfection: present spiritual attainment is imperfect — we know and prophesy only in part, mere portions of the full; the present; the imperfect; the in part; the portions; the incomplete. The witnesses observe Gill's guard: Gill notes this does not mean the Scriptures are imperfect (they are complete), nor that only some saints know — but that all the saints' knowledge here is partial; the guard; the Scriptures complete; the not some only; the all partial; the knowledge. The expositors note the full reserved for the world to come: the full understanding is reserved for the world to come (v.10, 12) — when the partial shall give way to the perfect; the full; the world to come; the perfect; the partial gives way; the reserved. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we know in part, the we prophesy in part, and the partial knowledge of the present. The verse thus records the partial nature of present knowledge — teaching the imperfection of present spiritual attainment, mere portions of the full understanding to come.
10:
"But when that which is perfect is come, then that which is in part shall be done away." The witnesses note the coming of the perfect and the passing of the partial. Gill notes "that which is perfect" is the perfect knowledge of God, Christ, and the mysteries of the kingdom, which shall take place not in this life, but in the life to come (the Jews placing it at the resurrection). The witnesses note Paul declaring that when that which is perfect is come, the partial shall be done away; the principle of the perfect displacing the imperfect — that when the perfect state arrives, the partial (knowledge, prophecy) shall vanish; the witnesses note the debate on "that which is perfect" — whether it is the eternal/heavenly state (the beatific vision at Christ's coming or the resurrection, the majority and Gill's reading), or, as some hold, the completion of the canon of Scripture (the cessationist reading); the perfect displacing the partial. The witnesses note that which is perfect is come. The witnesses note that which is in part shall be done away.

The witnesses note the perfect displacing the partial — when the perfect comes, the partial is done away; the debate on what "the perfect" is.

So the witnesses make the verse the perfect displacing the partial. The lesson the expositors draw is the principle that, when the perfect state arrives, the partial gifts shall vanish — with debate on what "the perfect" is. The witnesses note the perfect: "when that which is perfect is come" — Gill notes "that which is perfect" is the perfect knowledge of God and Christ, in the life to come; the perfect; the perfect knowledge; the come; the perfect state; the full. The expositors observe the passing: "then that which is in part shall be done away" — the partial (knowledge, prophecy) shall be done away; the passing; the in part; the done away; the vanish; the partial. The witnesses present the debate on "that which is perfect": the witnesses note the debate — the majority (and Gill) read "the perfect" as the eternal/heavenly state (the beatific vision at Christ's coming or the resurrection, when we see face to face, v.12); some (cessationist) read it as the completion of the canon of Scripture (when the partial gifts of revelation ceased); both agree the partial gives way to the perfect. The expositors draw the lesson on the perfect displacing the imperfect: when the perfect comes, the imperfect (the partial gifts) vanishes — as the dawn displaces the candle; the displacing; the perfect; the imperfect; the vanishes; the principle. The witnesses observe the majority reading: the majority read "the perfect" as the heavenly state — for v.12 ("face to face," "know even as I am known") points beyond the canon to the beatific vision; the majority; the heavenly state; the face to face; the beatific vision; the eternal. The expositors note the cessationist reading: some read "the perfect" as the completed Scripture — the partial revelatory gifts (prophecy, tongues, the word of knowledge) ceasing when the canon was complete; the cessationist; the completed Scripture; the gifts ceased; the canon; the reading. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that which is perfect is come, the that which is in part shall be done away, and the perfect displacing the partial. The verse thus records the perfect displacing the partial — teaching that, when the perfect state arrives, the partial gifts shall vanish.
11:
"When I was a child, I spake as a child, I understood as a child, I thought as a child: but when I became a man, I put away childish things." The witnesses note the illustration of the child and the man. Gill notes Paul illustrates the present gifts (as tongues), which in their highest exercise were but like the lisping of a child compared to what shall be known when the saints are perfect men in heaven. The witnesses note Paul illustrating the passing of the partial by the growth from child to man — as a child he spoke, understood, and thought as a child, but becoming a man, put away childish things; the figure of maturity displacing immaturity — that the present gifts and knowledge, however real, are to the coming perfection as a child's speech and thought to a grown man's, to be left behind when the believer reaches maturity in glory; the child and the man. The witnesses note I spake as a child. The witnesses note I put away childish things.

The witnesses note the figure of the child and the man — present gifts are to the coming perfection as a child's speech to a man's, to be left behind.

So the witnesses make the verse the figure of the child and the man. The lesson the expositors draw is that maturity displaces immaturity — the present gifts are to the coming perfection as a child's ways to a man's. The witnesses note the child: "when I was a child, I spake as a child, I understood as a child, I thought as a child" — Paul, as a child, spoke, understood, and thought as a child; the child; the spake; the understood; the thought; the immature. The expositors observe the man: "but when I became a man, I put away childish things" — becoming a man, he put away childish things; the man; the became; the put away; the childish things; the mature. The witnesses press the figure: Gill notes the present gifts (as tongues), in their highest exercise, are but like a child's lisping compared to the coming knowledge; the figure; the gifts; the child's lisping; the coming knowledge; the comparison. The expositors draw the lesson on maturity displacing immaturity: maturity displaces immaturity — the present gifts and knowledge, however real, are to the coming perfection as a child's speech to a man's; the maturity; the immaturity; the present; the coming; the displacing. The witnesses observe the gifts left behind: the present gifts are to be left behind when the believer reaches maturity in glory — as the man leaves the child's ways; the gifts left behind; the maturity; the glory; the leaving; the perfection. The expositors note the present as childhood: the present state (with its partial gifts) is as childhood — the coming state as manhood; the present childhood; the coming manhood; the partial; the perfect; the figure. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I spake as a child, the I put away childish things, and the child and the man. The verse thus records the figure of the child and the man — teaching that maturity displaces immaturity, the present gifts being to the coming perfection as a child's ways to a man's.
12:
"For now we see through a glass, darkly; but then face to face: now I know in part; but then shall I know even as also I am known." The witnesses note the contrast of present dimness and future clarity. Gill notes that now, in this life, the enlightened see God and the glory of Christ, but through a glass, darkly (as in a mirror, or an enigma); then they shall see face to face, in full and immediate vision. The witnesses note Paul contrasting the present dim sight (through a glass, darkly) with the future direct vision (face to face), and the present partial knowledge with the future knowledge "even as also I am known"; the great hope of the beatific vision — that the believer's present knowledge of God is dim and indirect (as a reflection in a clouded mirror), but in glory shall be clear, immediate, and full, knowing God as fully (in proportion) as God now knows him; the face-to-face vision. The witnesses note now we see through a glass, darkly. The witnesses note then face to face.

The witnesses note the contrast of present dimness and future clarity — now through a glass darkly, then face to face; the hope of the beatific vision.

So the witnesses make the verse the contrast of present dimness and future clarity. The lesson the expositors draw is the great hope of the beatific vision — present knowledge dim and indirect, future clear, immediate, and full. The witnesses note the present sight: "now we see through a glass, darkly" — Gill notes now we see God and Christ's glory, but through a glass, darkly (as in a mirror, or an enigma); the present sight; the through a glass; the darkly; the dim; the indirect. The expositors observe the future vision: "but then face to face" — then we shall see face to face (in full and immediate vision); the future vision; the face to face; the direct; the immediate; the clear. The witnesses press the present partial knowledge: "now I know in part" — now I know in part (partially, imperfectly); the partial knowledge; the in part; the imperfect; the present; the incomplete. The expositors draw the lesson on the future full knowledge: "but then shall I know even as also I am known" — then I shall know even as I am known (fully, in proportion, as God knows me); the future knowledge; the even as I am known; the full; the proportion; the complete. The witnesses observe the beatific vision: the great hope is the beatific vision — to see God face to face, clearly and immediately, in glory; the beatific vision; the face to face; the see God; the glory; the hope. The expositors note the present dimness: the present knowledge of God is dim and indirect — as a reflection in a clouded mirror, an enigma; the present dimness; the dim; the indirect; the mirror; the enigma. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the now we see through a glass darkly, the then face to face, and the face-to-face vision. The verse thus records the contrast of present dimness and future clarity — teaching the great hope of the beatific vision, present knowledge dim, future clear, immediate, and full.
13:
"And now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three; but the greatest of these is charity." The witnesses note the three abiding graces and the supremacy of love. Gill notes faith here is not the faith of miracles (which does not abide), but the faith peculiar to God's elect, which abides; and so hope and charity — the principal graces of the Spirit. The witnesses note Paul declaring that now abide faith, hope, and charity, these three, but the greatest is charity; the crowning conclusion — that against the transient gifts stand the three abiding graces (faith, hope, love), and that of these the greatest is love (charity), for it is the fulfilling of the law, the bond of perfectness, the grace that abides even in heaven when faith is sight and hope is fruition; the greatest of these is charity. The witnesses note now abideth faith, hope, charity. The witnesses note the greatest of these is charity.

The witnesses note the three abiding graces — faith, hope, charity — and the supremacy of love, the greatest, which abides even in heaven.

So the witnesses make the verse the three abiding graces and the supremacy of love. The lesson the expositors draw is that, against the transient gifts, stand faith, hope, and love, and the greatest of these is love. The witnesses note the abiding graces: "now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three" — Gill notes faith (not of miracles, but the elect's faith), hope, and charity abide — the principal graces; the abiding; the faith, hope, charity; the three; the graces; the permanent. The expositors observe the contrast with the gifts: against the transient gifts (prophecy, tongues, knowledge, which cease, v.8), stand the three abiding graces; the contrast; the transient gifts; the abiding graces; the cease and abide; the permanence. The witnesses press the supremacy of love: "the greatest of these is charity" — the greatest of the three is charity (love); the supremacy; the greatest; the charity; the love; the chief. The expositors draw the lesson on why love is greatest: love is greatest — for it is the fulfilling of the law (Romans 13:10), the bond of perfectness (Colossians 3:14), and the grace that abides even in heaven; the greatest; the fulfilling of the law; the bond of perfectness; the abides in heaven; the supreme. The witnesses observe love abiding in heaven: love abides even in heaven — when faith is turned to sight and hope to fruition, love remains and is perfected; the love abides; the heaven; the faith to sight; the hope to fruition; the love remains. The expositors note the crowning conclusion: the chapter crowns the argument — love, greater than the gifts and greater even than faith and hope, is the more excellent way (1 Corinthians 12:31); the crowning; the conclusion; the love greatest; the more excellent way; the supreme. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the now abideth faith hope charity, the greatest of these is charity, and the supremacy of love. The verse thus records the three abiding graces and the supremacy of love — teaching that, against the transient gifts, stand faith, hope, and love, and the greatest of these is love, which abides even in heaven.
